Home / EV Home Charging / Charging Costs & Efficiency

Three Real Utility Bills: What EV Charging Actually Added

Three realistic household bills — light driver, commuter family, two-EV home — dissected line by line to show exactly what EV charging adds and why.

EV Home Charging

By Louis F.

Electric Vehicle & Home Efficiency Specialist

July 8, 2026 6 min read

On this page

  1. Bill 1: The light driver (flat rate)
  2. Bill 2: The commuter family (TOU, scheduled)
  3. Bill 3: The two-EV household (EV rate plan)
  4. Reading your own bill like these
  5. Building your own bill preview
  6. What these bills would look like unoptimized
  7. The bills a year later
  8. Quick answers
  9. The pattern across all three

Calculators estimate; bills testify. The most convincing answer to “what will an EV do to my electric bill?” is watching the charging line appear in real household bills — so here are three realistic, fully worked cases spanning the common situations: a light-driving single-EV household, a commuter family, and a two-EV home on an EV rate plan. The numbers are illustrative composites built from the cost-per-mile method with typical rates, but the anatomy is exactly what you’ll see on your own bill.

Bill 1: The light driver (flat rate)

Retiree household, one efficient EV, ~450 miles/month, flat $0.15/kWh, no rate-plan change. Pre-EV baseline: 620 kWh, $93/month.

Bill 1: before and after
Pre-EVWith EVChange
Household kWh620620—
EV charging kWh (450 mi ÷ 3.6 mi/kWh × 1.12 loss)—140+140
Total620 kWh · $93760 kWh · $114+$21/mo

The takeaway: for a light driver, the EV is a ~$20 line item — visible, unremarkable, and replacing roughly $55 of gasoline for the same miles. No plan change, no schedule needed (flat rate), charging on Level 1 at that mileage if they choose. The gentlest possible version of EV ownership economics.

Bill 2: The commuter family (TOU, scheduled)

One EV crossover, ~1,100 miles/month at 3.1 mi/kWh, TOU plan ($0.32 peak / $0.17 shoulder / $0.11 off-peak), charging scheduled off-peak. Household baseline 850 kWh spread across periods.

Bill 2: where the charging landed
Household peak usage (180 kWh @ $0.32)$57.60
Household shoulder (450 kWh @ $0.17)$76.50
Household off-peak (220 kWh @ $0.11)$24.20
EV charging, all off-peak (398 kWh @ $0.11)$43.78
Total: $202 — of which the EV's 1,100 miles cost $43.78. The schedule put every charging kWh in the cheapest bucket.

Two lessons live in this bill. First, the EV added ~$44 for 1,100 miles — about 4 cents a mile, versus ~$130 of gas for the same driving. Second, notice the counterfactual: the same charging done carelessly at shoulder/peak hours would have cost $68–127. The one-time schedule is worth $25–80 on this single bill, every month, forever. This is also the bill where a first-month version would have looked scarier — unscheduled early charging landing in the expensive buckets before the habits set in.

Bill 3: The two-EV household (EV rate plan)

Sedan (1,000 mi @ 3.6) + SUV (750 mi @ 2.7), on an EV-specific plan with a $0.09 super-off-peak window, $0.18 day, $0.31 peak. Household baseline 900 kWh.

Bill 3: the fully-optimized two-EV bill
LinekWhCost
Household peak (170 @ $0.31)170$52.70
Household daytime (500 @ $0.18)500$90.00
Household off-peak (230 @ $0.09)230$20.70
Sedan charging (311 @ $0.09)311$27.99
SUV charging (311 @ $0.09)311$27.99
Total1,522$219.38
1,750 electric miles for $55.98 — while the plan's peak premium costs the household ~$18 vs flat on its fixed evening load. Net win: large.

The two-EV bill shows the second-EV economics in the wild: ~$56 fuels both cars (gas equivalent: ~$230), the deep overnight discount does the heavy lifting, and even the plan’s peak-premium cost on household usage is buried several times over by the charging savings. Note also the SUV’s higher per-mile share — same miles-class, visibly more kWh.

Reading your own bill like these

Three habits turn your bill into this kind of clarity: isolate the charging kWh (your charger app’s monthly total is the number — subtract it mentally from the bill’s total to see “the house” vs “the car”); check which bucket it landed in on TOU plans (the bill’s period breakdown reveals a silently failed schedule instantly — charging kWh in the peak bucket means the automation broke); and compare year-over-year, not month-over-month (seasonal heat and cold swings confound adjacent months; last July is this July’s honest benchmark).

Building your own bill preview

You can construct your version of these bills before the EV arrives, in ten minutes. Pull your last twelve utility bills (or the usage graphs in your utility’s portal) for the household baseline by season. Compute your expected charging kWh: monthly miles ÷ your candidate car’s efficiency × 1.12 for losses. Then place that kWh block into your current plan’s cheapest bucket (if TOU) or onto your flat rate, and — if considering an EV plan — re-price the whole stack under the candidate plan’s rates. The result is your Bill 2 or Bill 3, personalized. Doing this exercise before purchase does two things: it replaces the scary unknown with a boring number, and it reveals immediately whether a rate-plan switch belongs in your week-one setup or can wait.

What these bills would look like unoptimized

For honest contrast, run Bill 2’s counterfactual fully: the same commuter family, no schedule, charging on arrival at 6 p.m. through peak hours. The 398 charging kWh land mostly at $0.32 and $0.17 — roughly $95–110 instead of $43.78. Bill 3’s version is worse: two cars’ 622 kWh through an EV plan’s peak window would exceed $150 against the optimized $56. The gap between the optimized and careless versions of the same household — $50 to $95 every month — is larger than the gap between any two households in this article. That’s the recurring lesson of real bills: the rate plan and the schedule, not the car choice or the climate, are the biggest numbers on the page, and they’re the two things a ten-minute setup controls completely.

The bills a year later

Worth knowing how these three bills evolve: by year two, each household’s number is boring — and that’s the finding. Bill 1’s light driver sees ±$5 seasonal wobble on the $21 line. Bill 2’s family sees the winter premium push the $44 toward $55 in January and back by April. Bill 3’s two-EV home tracks its bucket concentration once a season and otherwise files the bill unread. No household reports the creeping-cost surprise that new owners fear, because there’s nothing to creep: rates change slowly and publicly, efficiency is stable, and the schedule doesn’t forget. The scary version of the EV utility bill exists almost entirely in month one — which is exactly why that month has its own guide.

Quick answers

Why don’t these bills show a separate “EV” line? Most utilities meter the whole house together — the “EV line” here is reconstructed from charger data. Separately-metered EV rates are the exception that itemizes it for real.

My charging kWh differs from my car’s “energy added” — which is right? Both — the gap is charging loss. The bill reflects the wall-side (larger) number.

What if my bill jumped more than these examples? Check the three usual suspects: unscheduled charging on TOU, a whole-house repricing you didn’t model, or season change arriving with the EV.

The pattern across all three

Strip the details and the same shape repeats: the EV adds a visible but modest line (roughly $20–60 per car for typical driving), the rate plan and schedule determine whether that line is as small as it can be, and the gasoline it displaces costs two to four times more in every single case. Bills don’t argue — and these are the bills.

Educational content, not financial advice — composites for illustration; your rates and miles are the real inputs. Reconstruct your own bill-to-be in the Sim-Lab.

Written by Louis F.

Electric Vehicle & Home Efficiency Specialist

A developer and digital creator dedicated to simplifying the intersection of electric vehicle ownership and home energy management.
